Reproductive Steroids, CAH & Intro to Endocrinology

Dr. Dave Johnson · UNE COM Block 5 · Week 40 OMK

PLOs × 4 LOs × 4 CAH Reproductive Steroids High-Yield
📑 Table of Contents PLO 1 – Testicular Testosterone Synthesis & Androgen Receptor PLO 2 – Central Regulation: GnRH, FSH, LH PLO 3 – Negative Feedback Control of Glandular Secretion PLO 4 – Hormone Binding to Plasma Proteins LO 1 – Pituitary Gland: Development, Structure, Vasculature & Relationship to Hypothalamus LO 2 – Components of the Hypothalamic-Hypophyseal-Gonadal Axis LO 3 – Age-Related Changes in HPG Axis → Puberty LO 4 – CAH: Phenotypic & Biochemical Differences, Altered Steroid Synthesis

PLO 1 · Testicular Synthesis of Testosterone & Androgen Receptor Binding

Steroidogenic Pathway Overview

All steroid hormones derive from cholesterol (C27). The initial rate-limiting step is cleavage of the cholesterol side chain by mitochondrial cytochrome P450scc (desmolase) to form pregnenolone (C21) — "the mother of all steroids."

Key Enzymes in Testosterone Synthesis (Testicular Leydig Cells)

In Leydig cells, LH binding to the LH receptor activates cAMP → stimulates desmolase → drives cholesterol → pregnenolone conversion. From there:

StepEnzymeSubstrate → ProductLocation
1P450scc (desmolase)Cholesterol → PregnenoloneMitochondria
217α-hydroxylasePregnenolone → 17α-OH-pregnenoloneSER
317,20-lyase17α-OH-pregnenolone → DHEASER
43β-HSDDHEA → AndrostenedioneSER
517β-HSDAndrostenedione → TestosteroneSER
65α-reductase (Type 2)Testosterone → DHT (most potent)Target tissues
7AromataseTestosterone → EstradiolPeripheral / Sertoli

SER = smooth endoplasmic reticulum. Mitochondrial enzymes are color-coded green, SER enzymes pink on Johnson's steroidogenic pathway figure.

Androgen Receptor Binding

Steroid hormones use intracellular/nuclear receptors, not cell-surface receptors. Testosterone (and DHT) diffuse across the plasma membrane → bind the androgen receptor (AR) in the cytoplasm → the hormone–receptor complex dimerizes and translocates to the nucleus → binds androgen response elements (AREs) on DNA → activates gene transcription → new protein synthesis.

The AR gene is located at Xq11-13 on the X chromosome (>200 known mutations → basis of Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome).

Relative Androgen Potency (Johnson's Table)

DHT = 100% > Testosterone (50%) > Androstenedione (8%) > DHEA (4%). DHT is the most potent natural androgen. 5α-reductase Type 2 converts testosterone → DHT in target tissues (prostate, external genitalia, hair follicles). Finasteride inhibits 5α-reductase Type 2 → used for BPH and male-pattern baldness.

PLO 2 · Central Regulation of Testosterone Synthesis: GnRH, FSH & LH

The Hypothalamic–Pituitary–Gonadal (HPG) Axis

Hypothalamus → secretes GnRH in pulsatile fashion into the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal vessels → reaches the anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis).

Anterior pituitary gonadotrophs respond to pulsatile GnRH by secreting:

• FSH → acts on Sertoli cells → supports spermatogenesis, produces inhibin and ABP (androgen-binding protein)

• LH → acts on Leydig cells → stimulates testosterone synthesis

Key concept from transcript: Pulsatile GnRH is required. Continuous GnRH (as with GnRH agonist administration) → desensitizes/downregulates GnRH receptors → decreases FSH/LH release (therapeutic principle for prostate cancer, precocious puberty).

PLO 3 · Control of Glandular Secretion by Negative Feedback

Types of Negative Feedback

"Long loop" negative feedback: End-organ hormone (e.g., testosterone, cortisol) feeds back to inhibit both the anterior pituitary and the hypothalamus.

"Short loop" negative feedback: Anterior pituitary hormone (e.g., ACTH, LH) feeds back to inhibit its own hypothalamic releasing hormone.

Clinical pearl (from transcript): In Classic CAH, cortisol cannot be produced adequately → no long-loop negative feedback on ACTH → ACTH levels rise dramatically → drives adrenal cortex proliferation (= the "hyperplasia" in CAH) and shunts precursors into androgen pathways.

Negative Feedback in the Male HPG Axis

• Testosterone → feeds back negatively at BOTH hypothalamus (↓ GnRH) and anterior pituitary (↓ LH release)

• Inhibin (from Sertoli cells) → feeds back negatively at anterior pituitary (↓ FSH only)

• Estradiol (aromatized from testosterone) → also feeds back at hypothalamus to ↓ GnRH

PLO 4 · Significance of Hormone Binding to Plasma Proteins

Why Plasma Protein Binding Matters

Steroids are lipophilic → they cannot dissolve easily in aqueous plasma → they travel bound to carrier proteins. Only the free (unbound) fraction is biologically active — it can cross cell membranes and bind intracellular receptors.

SteroidPrimary CarrierNotes
Testosterone / DHTSHBG (sex hormone-binding globulin)~98% bound; also binds albumin
CortisolCBG (cortisol-binding globulin / transcortin)~90–95% bound
AldosteroneAlbumin (weak)~50% bound → shorter t₁/₂
Estradiol / ProgesteroneSHBG and albuminEstradiol binds SHBG with lower affinity than T

Clinical significance: Changes in binding protein levels alter total hormone levels but NOT free hormone. Example: Pregnancy or OCP use → ↑ hepatic SHBG production → ↑ total testosterone but free testosterone may be unchanged. When clinicians need to evaluate true androgenic status, they measure free testosterone.

In a clinical workup distinguishing primary synthesis changes from peripheral conversion changes, it is important to know the binding status (Johnson's Repro Steroids Slide 8).

LO 1 · Pituitary Gland: Development, Structure, Vasculature & Hypothalamic Relationship

Two Lobes of the Pituitary

Anterior lobe (adenohypophysis) — derived from Rathke's pouch (oral ectoderm). Contains hormone-secreting cells. Receives hypothalamic input indirectly via the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal vessels.

Posterior lobe (neurohypophysis) — derived from neural ectoderm (diencephalon). Contains axon terminals of magnocellular neurons from the supraoptic (ADH) and paraventricular (oxytocin) nuclei. ADH & oxytocin are "neurohormones" because they are synthesized in hypothalamic neuron cell bodies but released from axon terminals in the posterior pituitary.

Hypothalamo-Hypophyseal Portal System

Parvocellular neurons in the hypothalamus secrete releasing/inhibiting hormones into the median eminence → picked up by the first capillary bed → travel down portal vessels → reach a second capillary bed in the anterior pituitary → stimulate or inhibit hormone-secreting cells there.

Critical concept (Johnson emphasizes): These portal vessels are essential because hypothalamic releasing hormones exist in such small quantities that they would be diluted to ineffective concentrations if released into the general circulation. The portal system ensures high local concentrations reach the anterior pituitary.

The 6 + 2 Pituitary Hormones

Adenohypophysis (6): GH, ACTH, TSH, FSH, LH, Prolactin (+ MSH from intermediate lobe remnant)

Neurohypophysis (2): ADH (vasopressin) and Oxytocin

LO 2 · Components of the Hypothalamic-Hypophyseal-Gonadal Axis

Three-Level Architecture

Level 1 — Hypothalamus: GnRH neurons secrete GnRH in pulsatile bursts (the "circhoral oscillator") → enter portal vessels at the median eminence

Level 2 — Anterior Pituitary: Gonadotroph cells respond to GnRH pulses → secrete FSH & LH into systemic circulation

Level 3 — Gonads:

• Testes: LH → Leydig cells → testosterone; FSH → Sertoli cells → spermatogenesis support, inhibin, ABP

• Ovaries: LH → theca cells → androgens; FSH → granulosa cells → aromatize androgens to estradiol

Localizing Endocrine Disease (Johnson's Framework)

LevelDisease TypeExample
Peripheral glandPrimaryPrimary hypogonadism (testicular failure) → ↓T, ↑LH/FSH
PituitarySecondaryPituitary adenoma → ↓FSH/LH → ↓T
HypothalamusTertiaryKallmann syndrome (↓GnRH) → ↓FSH/LH → ↓T

LO 3 · Age-Related Changes in the HPG Axis → Puberty

Activation of the HPG Axis at Puberty

Before puberty, the GnRH pulse generator is relatively quiescent — low-amplitude, infrequent pulses keep gonadotropin levels low. At puberty onset:

1. Increased frequency & amplitude of pulsatile GnRH release (initially during sleep, then throughout the day)

2. → ↑ FSH & LH secretion from the anterior pituitary

3. → Gonadal activation: in males, Leydig cells begin producing significant testosterone; Sertoli cells begin supporting spermatogenesis

4. → Secondary sexual characteristics develop (deep voice, body/pubic/axillary hair, muscle mass, penile growth, skeletal maturation)

Estrogen's role in growth plates: Regardless of genetic sex, it is predominantly estrogen (often aromatized from androgens) that drives epiphyseal plate fusion. This is why excess androgens in CAH → excess estrogen → premature fusion → short adult stature.

Central vs. Non-Central Precocious Puberty

Definitions (from Johnson's slides)

Central Precocious Puberty (CPP) = early activation of the HPG axis → true puberty before age 8 in girls / 9 in boys. The androgens come from activated gonads.

Non-Central (Peripheral) Precocious Puberty (NCPP) = pubertal signs NOT driven by HPG axis activation. Androgens come from other sources: adrenal cortex (CAH), tumors, exogenous hormones, McCune-Albright. Also called "pseudo-precocious puberty".

How to Distinguish CPP from NCPP in Males (from transcript — CBL prep)

TestCentral (true) PPNon-Central PP (e.g., CAH)
GnRH stimulation testHuge surge in LH ~1 hr after infusion (HPG axis is active)No significant LH response (HPG axis is quiescent; androgens are adrenal)
Testicular volumeEnlarged for age (testes are active & producing T)Prepubertal / small for degree of virilization (testes are not the source)
17-OHP levelNormalElevated (in 21-OHD CAH)
ACTH stimulation testNormal cortisol riseExaggerated 17-OHP rise; blunted cortisol rise
If an 8-year-old XY male presents with signs of hyperandrogenism, what two diagnoses should be at the top of your differential? True central precocious puberty vs. non-classic CAH. Use GnRH stimulation and testicular volume to distinguish them.

LO 4 · CAH: Phenotypic & Biochemical Differences & Altered Steroid Synthesis

Overview of Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia

CAH = a group of autosomal recessive disorders involving deficiency of an enzyme in the synthesis of cortisol, aldosterone, or both. 95% of cases are due to 21α-hydroxylase deficiency (CYP21A2 gene mutations; ~300 known mutations).

Prevalence: ~1 in 15,000 live births worldwide (neonatal screening data).

Why "Hyperplasia"?

21α-hydroxylase deficiency → ↓ cortisol → loss of negative feedback at hypothalamus/pituitary → ↑↑ CRH → ↑↑ ACTH → ACTH drives adrenal cortex proliferation (hyperplasia) → ACTH also drives cholesterol → pregnenolone conversion, flooding the pathway with precursors that cannot cross the 21α-hydroxylase block → precursors are shunted into androgen pathways.

Classic vs. Non-Classic CAH

Updated Classification (2024 — from Johnson's slides citing UpToDate)

FeatureClassic CAHNon-Classic (Late-Onset) CAH
SeveritySevere enzyme deficiencyMild/partial enzyme deficiency
OnsetAt birth / neonatalChildhood / adolescence / adulthood
CortisolMarkedly deficient → adrenal insufficiencyMildly low; sufficient for normal function (no adrenal crisis)
AldosteroneMay be deficient (salt wasting) — degree variesUsually adequate
AndrogensMarkedly elevated → ambiguous genitalia in XX femalesMildly elevated → precocious puberty, hirsutism, acne
17-OHP>300 nmol/L (1000 ng/mL) in neonates200–1500 ng/dL; >1500 ng/dL after ACTH stim = diagnostic
Neonatal screeningDetected (mandated heel-stick)Usually NOT detected by neonatal screening

2024 update: The old subdivision of Classic CAH into "salt-wasting" vs. "simple virilizing" has fallen out of favor because phenotypes overlap (all lose some salt; all XX females have atypical genitalia). Now just: Classic or Non-Classic.

Presentation by Genetic Sex

Classic CAH in XX Females

Excess adrenal androgens in utero → the genital tubercle, urogenital groove, and labioscrotal folds (present in all fetuses by week 7) become virilized:

• Clitoromegaly (can resemble a penis)

• Partial to complete fusion of labioscrotal folds (can resemble a scrotum)

→ Born with ambiguous external genitalia

CRITICAL: Internal reproductive organs are NORMAL. No Sertoli cells → no MIS → Müllerian ducts persist → normal uterus, fallopian tubes, cervix, and ovaries. The virilization is ONLY external.

Classic CAH in XY Males

External genitalia appear normal at birth (testes already produce androgens → additional adrenal androgens don't create a visually abnormal phenotype). May have subtly enlarged phallus but usually not enough to prompt workup. Diagnosis relies on neonatal screening (heel-stick 17-OHP).

Without screening → potentially fatal adrenal crisis (cortisol + aldosterone deficiency) within first weeks of life.

Non-Classic CAH Presentation (Both Sexes)

Children present with signs of hyperandrogenism without adrenal insufficiency:

• Precocious pubic hair / axillary hair

• Clitoromegaly (females) or penile enlargement (males)

• Accelerated growth velocity with advanced bone age

• Medication-resistant acne

• Short adult stature (excess androgens → excess estrogen via aromatase → premature epiphyseal fusion)

Key point (from transcript): These patients DO have mild cortisol/aldosterone deficiency, but it's subclinical under normal conditions. However, during severe illness/stress, they may not mount an adequate cortisol response → may need stress-dose glucocorticoid coverage in the hospital.

Other Enzyme Deficiencies in CAH

11β-hydroxylase deficiency: Block at 11-deoxycortisol → cortisol. ↑↑ 11-deoxycortisol and deoxycorticosterone. Also shunts to excess androgens. (Much rarer than 21-OHD.)

3β-HSD deficiency: Block at pregnenolone → progesterone pathway. Abnormal ratio of 17-OH-pregnenolone to 17-OHP and of DHEA to androstenedione. Also can produce excess androgens.

Both can cause cortisol ± aldosterone deficiency with excess androgen production, but are far less common than 21α-hydroxylase deficiency.

Treatment of CAH

Treatment Principles

Classic CAH — lifelong treatment:

1. Glucocorticoid replacement — replaces missing cortisol AND provides negative feedback to ↓ ACTH → ↓ adrenal androgen production

2. Mineralocorticoid replacement (fludrocortisone) — if aldosterone deficiency present

3. Aromatase inhibitor — slows skeletal maturation by preventing androgen → estrogen conversion → protects growth potential

4. Androgen receptor antagonists (flutamide, bicalutamide) — reduce virilization effects

NEW (2024): Crenessity (crinecerfont) — FDA approved for classic CAH in adults and children ≥4 years. Mechanism: blocks CRF Type 1 receptor in the anterior pituitary → ↓ ACTH release → ↓ adrenal androgen overproduction. Major benefit: allows lower glucocorticoid doses (reducing long-term side effects of chronic high-dose GC therapy).

In Classic CAH, why does giving exogenous glucocorticoid help reduce androgen levels? Because it restores the missing negative feedback at the hypothalamus/pituitary → ↓ CRH/ACTH → less drive on the adrenal cortex → less shunting of precursors into androgen pathways.
46,XX Classic CAH female: What internal reproductive structures does she have at birth? Normal cervix, uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries. MIS is absent (no Sertoli cells) → Müllerian ducts persist normally.
